title: Job 15
subtitle: Eliphaz’s Second Speech: Harsher

heb.paragraph: "Should the wise fill their bellies with the hot east wind (rūaḥ qādîm)?" (v.2). Eliphaz accuses Job of producing only hot air. The east wind (qādîm) is the sirocco — the dry, scorching desert wind that kills vegetation. Job’s words, Eliphaz says, are destructive, not wise.

refs.note: "When words are many, sin is not absent" — the proverbial basis for Eliphaz’s suspicion of many words.
Compare Eliphaz’s gentle first opening with this aggressive second one.

notes.note: Eliphaz attacks Job’s speech as worthless wind. The tone shift from chapter 4 is dramatic.

"You even undermine piety and hinder devotion to God. Your sin prompts your mouth; you adopt the tongue of the crafty. Your own mouth condemns you." Eliphaz claims Job’s protests are evidence of guilt.
"Were you the first man born? Were you brought forth before the hills?" Eliphaz challenges Job’s wisdom. You are not primordial; you have no special knowledge.
"What are mortals, that they could be pure?" Eliphaz repeats his night vision theology (4:17): all humans are corrupt. This time without the vision — just the assertion.
Calvin: “your own mouth condemns you” — Eliphaz treats Job’s protests as confessions. This is circular reasoning: complaining about unjust suffering proves you deserve it.
Calvin: the universal corruption argument is theologically valid (Rom 3:23) but pastorally useless. Telling a sufferer that everyone is guilty answers nothing.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: the “east wind” (qādîm) is the sirocco — the hot, dry wind from the Arabian desert that desiccates everything. Eliphaz says Job’s words are equally destructive.
The NET: “were you the first man born?” echoes the Primeval Man tradition — the first human was thought to have special wisdom by virtue of proximity to creation.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: “you undermine piety” is Eliphaz’s most serious charge. Job’s protest, in the friends’ view, is not just personal error but a public danger — it teaches others to question God.
Clines: “were you the first man born?” may allude to the Primeval Man myth (cf. Ezek 28:12–15). Eliphaz denies Job any special access to divine wisdom.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: the prosecution reopens its case. Eliphaz introduces new charges: Job’s words themselves are evidence of guilt. His protests undermine public religion. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: Eliphaz has dropped all gentleness. In his first speech (ch 4–5) he began with compliments. Now he leads with “your own mouth condemns you” (v.6). His argument: Job’s protests undermine piety itself, not just Job’s case. By challenging God, Job is discouraging others from faith.

sections.header: Verses 17–35 — The Fate of the Wicked

heb.paragraph: "The wicked person (rāshāʿ) writhes in pain all their days" (v.20). Eliphaz paints an elaborate portrait of the wicked person’s fate. The implication is unmistakable: this is your future, Job, unless you repent.

refs.note: The Psalmist saw the prosperity of the wicked and was troubled — until he entered the sanctuary. Eliphaz only sees the punishment side.
"They will eat the fruit of their ways" — the proverbial tradition Eliphaz draws from.

notes.note: The wicked: tormented all their days, terrifying sounds in their ears, destruction waits. "He shakes his fist at God and vaunts himself against the Almighty."
Obesity, ruined cities, lost wealth. Eliphaz connects physical comfort with spiritual corruption. The rich wicked will lose everything.
"The company of the godless will be barren... they conceive trouble and give birth to evil." The speech ends with a retribution image: the wicked produce only destruction.
Calvin: the wicked do suffer, and Eliphaz’s descriptions are not wrong as general observations. They are wrong as a diagnosis of Job’s specific case.
Calvin: accusing Job of shaking his fist at God is Eliphaz’s worst misreading. Job has not shaken his fist; he has cried for a hearing. Protest and rebellion are different things.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: “writhes in pain all their days” uses a word for the physical contortions of labour pains. The wicked’s entire life is agony.
The NET: the closing image of conceiving trouble and giving birth to evil (cf. Isa 59:4) frames wickedness as generative — it produces more of itself.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the catalogue of wicked-person horrors is aimed at Job. Each line is a mirror: you live in terror, you wander, you will lose everything. Eliphaz is too “polite” to say “you are wicked” directly — he paints the portrait and lets Job see himself in it.
Clines: “shakes his fist at God” — Eliphaz implies Job’s protests are rebellion against God. The legal complaint has been redefined as insubordination.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: Eliphaz presents a precedent catalogue — here is what happens to the wicked. The unspoken conclusion: Job’s experience matches the pattern, therefore Job is wicked. Habel's form-critical analysis identifies the passage's literary type and rhetorical function within the book's larger structure. The interplay of lament, wisdom disputation, and divine speech creates a multi-voiced exploration of theodicy that resists reduction to any single theological position.

hist.context: The second half of Eliphaz’s speech is a long catalogue of horrors awaiting the wicked. The wicked live in terror (v.20–21), wander hungry (v.23), are crushed (v.24), defied God (v.25–26), and lose everything (vv.29–34). The description bears uncomfortable resemblance to Job’s actual situation — which is precisely Eliphaz’s rhetorical strategy.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVBut you even undermine piety and hinder devotion to God.ESVBut you are doing away with the fear of God and hindering meditation before God.NIVYour sin prompts your mouth; you adopt the tongue of the crafty.ESVFor your iniquity teaches your mouth, and you choose the tongue of the crafty.

rec.text: "Eliphaz attacks Job’s speech as worthless wind. The tone shift from chapter 4 is dramatic."

rows.label: The Accusation — 15:1–16
rows.text: “Your own mouth condemns you.” The gloves are off.

rows.label: The Portrait — 15:17–35
rows.text: The wicked’s fate: terror, hunger, ruin. A mirror held up to Job.

hebtext.note: "Should the wise fill their bellies with the hot east wind (rūaḥ qādîm)?" (v.2). Eliphaz accuses Job of producing only hot air. The east wind (qādîm) is the sirocco — the dry, scorching desert wind that kills vegetation. Job’s words, Eliphaz says, are destructive, not wise.

"The wicked person (rāshāʿ) writhes in pain all their days" (v.20). Eliphaz paints an elaborate portrait of the wicked person’s fate. The implication is unmistakable: this is your future, Job, unless you repent.

thread.text: "When words are many, sin is not absent" — the proverbial basis for Eliphaz’s suspicion of many words.
Compare Eliphaz’s gentle first opening with this aggressive second one.
The Psalmist saw the prosperity of the wicked and was troubled — until he entered the sanctuary. Eliphaz only sees the punishment side.
"They will eat the fruit of their ways" — the proverbial tradition Eliphaz draws from.

themes.note: Judgment peaks: Eliphaz’s second speech is pure condemnation. Justice: the retribution catalogue is applied directly to Job.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on Eliphaz's tone growing harsher — accusing Job of undermining piety — consider asking God whether you become more aggressive when your arguments fail to persuade, and whether volume has ever substituted for truth in your speech.

coaching.tip: Second dialogue cycle begins. The gloves come off. Eliphaz abandons gentle appeal for direct accusation: 'Are you the first man ever born?' (v. 7). He mocks Job's claim to wisdom and accuses him of 'crafty' speech (v. 5). The pastoral pretense is gone. Job's resistance has exhausted their patience.

Eliphaz's portrait of the wicked (vv. 20-35) is meant as warning, but Job will later turn these images against the friends themselves. The wicked 'shakes his fist at God' — but Job openly challenges God and isn't destroyed. The wicked lives in terror — but Job's friends claim the righteous prosper. Whose experience matches reality?
